revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:縹]][[zh:縹色]]
'''Hanada''' (縹) or '''Hanada-iro''' (縹色, 花田色) refers to a light blue color with high brightness. According to a dictionary from the Later Han Dynasty, "縹" (Hanada) is synonymous with "漂" (a pale blue color). Other names include '''Hana-iro''' (花色), '''Tsukikusa-iro''' (月草色), '''Chigusa-iro''' (千草色), and '''Tsuyukusa-iro''' (露草色), all of which refer to the {{ill|Commelina communis|en|Commelina communis|qid=Q159111|lt=Commelina|1=Commelina communis}} plant (dayflower). However, there is also a separate color known as {{ill|Chigusa-iro (color)|ja|千草色|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chigusa-iro|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11405851|1=Chigusa-iro}} (千種色).
== Hanada-iro == Originally, Hanada-iro referred to the color obtained from squeezing the petals of {{ill|Tsuyukusa|ja|露草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tsuyukusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ツユクサ|1=Commelina communis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q159111}} (dayflower) for use as a dye. However, this blue color fades extremely easily and disappears when exposed to water, so it was commonly replaced with a more durable indigo dye derived from {{ill|Persicaria tinctoria|en|Persicaria tinctoria|qid=Q11833922|lt=Ai (plant)|1=Persicaria tinctoria}} (Japanese indigo). In ancient times, Hanada was also used as a general term for blue hues. Nevertheless, cultivated forms of Tsuyukusa, such as {{ill|Ōbōshibana|ja|オオボウシバナ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōbōshibana|1=Commelina communis var. hortensis|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q7338833}}, are still used today in {{ill|Yūzen|en|Yūzen|qid=Q13377707|lt=Yūzen dyeing|1=Yūzen}} for underpainting designs.
The *Kojiki* tells a story of an envoy sent by {{ill|Emperor Nintoku|en|Emperor Nintoku|qid=Q313119|lt=Emperor Nintoku|1=Emperor Nintoku}} who, after being rejected by the empress, continued to plead underground until the blue dye from his clothes flowed into the puddles. This suggests that lower-Ranking officials wore garments dyed with such delicate colors. Even in later times, court rituals required attendants to wear garments dyed with {{ill|Yama-ai|ja|山藍|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yama-ai|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (mountain indigo) or Tsuyukusa in accordance with ancient customs.
In contrast, the *Hanada* used in ancient clothing systems such as the {{ill|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|en|Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|qid=Q2448809|lt=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System|1=Twelve Level Cap and Rank System}} was a robust indigo dye. This durable blue was worn as the regular uniform for officials of the corresponding Rank.
By the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}, Tsuyukusa was no longer commonly used for dyeing clothing. However, *Hana-iro* (flower color) became synonymous with impermanence. In *The Pillow Book*, it is lamented that "a color that fades too quickly is unfortunate," referring to the fleeting blue of Tsuyukusa.
Later, Tsuyukusa-dyeing remained primarily for underpainting, while *Hana-iro* in the {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|qid=Q184963|lt=Edo period|1=Edo period}} referred to a practical, indigo-dyed cotton fabric commonly used as kimono lining.
In the {{ill|Japanese court Ranks and titles|ja|位階|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Japanese court Ranks and titles|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Court Ranks in Japan|qid=Q99196082}} system, officials of the sixth Rank wore *Hanada*-colored robes, leading to court servants (who were typically of the sixth Rank) being called *Ao-zamurai* (青侍, "blue samurai").
== {{Anchors|Deep Hanada|Medium Hanada|Next Hanada|Light Hanada|White Hanada}} Deep Hanada, Medium Hanada, Next Hanada, Light Hanada, and White Hanada == {{ Infobox subcolor|title=Deep Hanada|hex=274765| kana=ふかきはなだ |english= | }} {{ Infobox subcolor|title=Medium Hanada|hex=3F729F| kana=なかのはなだ |english= | }} {{ Infobox subcolor|title=Next Hanada|hex=618BAD| kana=つぎのはなだ |english= | }} {{ Infobox subcolor|title=Light Hanada|hex=67A7CC| kana=あさきはなだ |english= | }} {{ Infobox subcolor|title=White Hanada|hex=A3D8F6| kana=しろきはなだ |english= | }}
In the mid-Heian period legal code *Engishiki*, *Hanada-iro* was classified into four shades: *Deep Hanada* (深縹, Fuka-hanada), *Medium Hanada* (中縹, Naka-hanada), *Next Hanada* (次縹, Tsugi-hanada), and *Light Hanada* (浅縹, Asa-hanada), arranged from darkest to lightest. Deep Hanada was a deep indigo shade approaching black. The most commonly recognized *Hanada-iro* corresponds to Medium Hanada.
Additionally, *Engishiki* also mentions *White Hanada* (白縹, Shiro-hanada), which is even paler than Light Hanada.
== Pale Hanada == {{ Infobox subcolor|title=Pale Hanada|hex=9BC9D5| kana=うすはなだ |english= | }}
Apart from Light Hanada and White Hanada, there is also a shade called *Pale Hanada* (薄縹, Usu-hanada), which is a subdued, slightly grayish light blue.
== Tsuyukusa-iro and Hana-iro == {{Infobox color|title=Tsuyukusa-iro|hex=21A0DB | kana=つゆくさいろ |english= | r=33|g=160|b=219 | c=73 |m=21 |y=0 |k=0 | h=199|s=85|v=86 | munsell=3PB 5/dd}} {{Infobox jiscolor | title=Tsuyukusa-iro | hex=3D7CCE | munsell=3PB 5/dd }} {{Infobox color|title=Hana-iro|hex=426AB3 | kana=はないろ |english= | r=66|g=106|b=179 | c=80 |m=60 |y=0 |k=0 | h=219|s=63|v=70 | munsell=6.5PB 4/10}}
[[File:Two-blue-petals bgiu.jpg|left|thumb|200px|Tsuyukusa flower]]
{{Anchors|Tsuyukusa-iro|Chigusa-iro|Tsukikusa-iro}} *Tsuyukusa-iro* (露草色) refers to the natural color of the Tsuyukusa flower. Originally, it was synonymous with *Hanada-iro*, but as *Hanada-iro* became a general term for blue hues, *Tsuyukusa-iro* came to represent a lighter, more vivid blue. Since *Chigusa* (千草) and *Tsukikusa* (月草) are alternate names for Tsuyukusa, *Chigusa-iro* and *Tsukikusa-iro* also likely refer to this shade.
{{Anchors|Hana-iro}} *Hana-iro* (花色) is another color with the same origin as *Hanada-iro* and *Tsuyukusa-iro*. The term originally derives from *Hanada-iro*’s alternate name *Hanada-iro* (花田色), abbreviated to *Hana-iro*. This originally referred to the blue of the Tsuyukusa flower and was identical to *Hanada-iro*. Although *Hana-iro* is sometimes still considered equivalent to *Hanada-iro*, over time it came to denote a slightly more purplish shade of blue.
However, *Hana-iro* is now used broadly beyond its original definition. It may refer to colors ranging from deep red to pale pink, or even yellow, as it is sometimes interpreted simply as "flower color." Because most flowers in Japan are not blue, and the default association for "flower" is *sakura*, this shift in meaning occurred.
== See Also == * {{ill|Color|en|Color|qid=Q1075|lt=Colors|1=Color}} * {{ill|Lists of colors|en|Lists of colors|qid=Q6626594|lt=List of colors|1=Lists of colors}} * {{ill|Traditional colors of Japan|en|Traditional colors of Japan|qid=Q2607820|lt=List of traditional Japanese colors|1=Traditional colors of Japan}}
